item.title: Keep Android Open
item.description: Your phone is about to stop being yours. In September 2026, Google will block every Android app whose developer hasn't registered with them.

item.title: An update on GitHub availability
item.description: Here’s what we’ve done—and what we’re still doing—to improve our availability and reliability.

item.title: GTFOBins
item.description: GTFOBins is a curated list of Unix-like executables that can be used to bypass local security restrictions in misconfigured systems.

item.title: Introducing talkie: a 13B vintage language model from 1930
item.description: April 2026

item.title: Soniferous Aether
SUPER ZSNES
item.description: A GPU-powered SNES emulator rewritten from scratch with hi-res Mode 7, per-game enhancements, and a modernized classic UI.

item.title: To my students
item.description: There have been times, especially this year, when I wonder despairingly what it is exactly that I am preparing you for. The software industry is going completely insane, not to mention the political climate. It feels almost unethical to train you as computer scientists only to send you out into a world where entry-level computing jobs are difficult to find; where intellectual property is not respected; where code quantity is valued over quality, and short-term profits over long-term sustainability; where technology is used to distract, extract, surveil, and kill, and designed to exploit some of our deepest cognitive biases and blind spots; where centuries of bias and discrimination are enshrined in systems trained on biased data; where scarce resources are consumed by profligate use of computing for uncertain benefits; where people are racing to create intelligent machines, but only in order to make them slaves.

item.title: Don't use localhost:3000, use your own custom domain
item.description: After presenting a demo of how an internal tool works, I was flooded with questions. Not about the tool, but about why I had bought a domain just to run the demo. "Why didn't you use the staging serve

item.title: The One-Person Stack | ivan.codes
item.description: How to ship real products alone and what to care about at each stage.

item.title: We Are Not Language Machines
item.description: The nightmare of materialism, which has turned the life of the universe into an evil, useless game, is not yet past; it holds the awakening soul still in its grip. - Wassily Kandinsky

item.title: Good Design vs. Bad Design
item.description: Most designers will talk to you about fonts, colors, and layouts. Few will ever tell you what design actually is — and why that misunderstanding costs everyone.
item.author: (https://thomasmcgee.co)

item.title: Building a SQL Analyst Agent
item.description: How to build a simple AI SQL analyst agent with Node.js, OpenAI, Vercel AI SDK, and SQLite.
item.author: (Ramin Mousavi)

item.title: Notes on Serial Experiments Lain by Jordan Matthiass
item.description: Notes on Serial Experiments Lain, the Wired, fractured selfhood, alienation, and the digital apocalypse that came true.

item.title: Exponentially Smoothed FPS Counters
item.description: Exploring how to apply an interesting numerical technique with constant time and space complexity to FPS counters.

item.title: I Spent My Sabbatical Building a Power Meter for Sledgehammers
item.description: One month, one prototype — the first Intensity Pad, and why I think striking movements deserve a power meter of their own.
item.author: (https://leblancfg.com/author/francois-leblanc.html)

item.title: Your blog is a radio station - Down the Road
item.description: A useful metaphor for us niche bloggers that helps explain why some readers stay.

item.title: A Brief History of SQL: From 1970 to 2026
item.description: SQL was created in 1973, survived NoSQL, and 55.6% of devs use PostgreSQL today. The full history with standards, tools, and why SQL won.

item.title: Softmax, can you really derive the Jacobian? And should you care? — idlemachines
item.description: Practice machine learning engineering with hands-on coding challenges. Implement neural network components, backprop, and more — from scratch.

item.title: The cost of being "nice"
item.description: The other day, a link popped up on Hacker News reviving this old Reddit post1 in which u/flipstables shares a pretty long and honest list of things they learned as a senior engineer. I highly suggest going through it as most of the things there are pure gold. But one, seemingly mundane, item inspired me to write this chunk: Be kind to everyone. Not because it’ll help your career (it will), but because being kind is rewarding by itself.

item.title: Most People Who Ride Bikes Have Soft Tires
item.description: A look at the quietest barrier to bicycle adoption (inflating your tires), and why bike share and public pumps deserve more credit than they get.

item.title: ChatGPT Images 2.0 Still Can't Draw the Seven-legged Spider I Want
item.description: ChatGPT Images 2.0 is able to generate a seven-legged spider, but it's not missing the leg I asked for.

item.title: steer coding agents with interactive diagrams — Will Hennessy
item.description: The Claude Architect plugin adds an interactive architecture diagram to Plan Mode so you can review, annotate, and revise the plan with Claude in real time.

item.title: You can beat the binary search
item.description: We sometimes have to look for a value in a sorted array. The simplest algorithm consists in just going through the values one by one, until we encounter the value, or exhaust the array. We sometimes call this algorithm a linear search. In C++, you can get the desired effect with the std::find function. For … Continue reading You can beat the binary search
item.author: (https://lemire.me/blog/author/lemire/)

item.title: Uptime golf
item.description: I’ve been noticing a lot of service outages lately. Some with few enough nines that you’d think they were going for a low score. My guess: this is probably going to get worse before it gets better. But maybe not for the reasons you’d think.

item.title: LLMs and the Adversarial Loop
item.description: LLMs are confident. So much so that if you ask one to give you the recipe to immortality, it will probably take a stab at it, even though it knows as much about mortality as it does its own existence. Ask one to write a design spec and it’ll produce something that reads well, covers the obvious cases, and sounds authoritative. Ask it to implement code from that spec and it’ll write something that compiles and handles the happy path. But that’s as good as it gets.
item.author: (Dale Myers)

item.title: Why You Can't Write That Book
item.description: I could do that, I just need more time.

item.title: Cost of PostgreSQL performance issues | Stormatics
item.description: Understand the cost of PostgreSQL performance issues. Learn how query inefficiency, bloat, and misconfiguration drive unnecessary spend.

item.title: Postgres's lateral joins allow for quite the good eDSL
item.description: ../postgres-lateral-makes-quite-a-good-dsl
